item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…instead of being calculated in a numeric value up to 2400, the results instead take the form of a form letter. The form letter informs the the Test Taker…

Double "instead"s, double "the"s (I've bolded), and the form of a form letter which form letter informs.

So this sentence was clearly brought to us by the Department of Redundancy Department :)

The fix is too big to count as routine grammar correction, particularly since the current form is +100. But I suggest replacing the quoted section with:

"..instead of being calculated in a numeric value up to 2400, the results consist of a form letter. The letter informs the Test Taker…

Also, this section specifies only two possible results when there are three. But that's even closer to a pure style choice than the other proposed edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minus the issue of having censorship in the containment section, I really enjoyed reading this. Very unique idea.

Although, it didn't make a lot of sense to be that someone is a "[EXPUNGED] fuck". Like, what on earth could have been worse to call him?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote. It was good, but you have censorship in the special containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article made me feel like I If i was reading an article before join the Foundation. +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP clearly passes the test
Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has several problems that honestly should have stopped me from upvoting, but I liked it the execution enough that I upvoted anyway. Author, I hope you take a second look at this, though (particularly the stuff Rhett pointed out).

Bothers me enough on second read that I'm downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't care for it, especially the interview. Definitely an OTT, and the whole "euclid!" "denied!" "but you have to, see?" "ok!" is always tiresome to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just read it; I didn't mean to take anyone's idea.

Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, my entry on SCP-914's test page hasn't disappeared. Ctrl+F GCSE on there. You'll see something rather like this, except the whole "fail at life" bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it and am upvoting. In part because I had an SCP draft that did a similar thing, and I still like the idea. One issue: you have got to do something more with the part about people who pass. Over-expungement really ruins that part for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's because I never really cared for test results, but this one does nothing for me.

When not being used, SCP-1071-1 and -2 are ordinary SAT testing materials distributed from the year 200█ onwards. Reading through them without meeting the activation criteria shows that the questions consist of several mathematics, literature, history, science, and language questions, as expected.

Attempts to observe the contents of SCP-1071-1 and -2 during the duration of the test are futile; the paper appears blank to all outside observers.

Why does it needs to be blank? Couldn't it continue to show ordinary apropriate questions?

More importantly, what happens after the test? Can the answers or questions be seen?

appears in the Test Taker's current place of residence, regardless of whether or not there is a mailbox, or even a mail delivery service, available. SCP-1071-3 can only be opened by the Test Taker,

Is this really necessary? What's wrong with someone else opening it, or test taker not receving it due to post sevice error.

And the rest is just an OTT exam. "Congratulations. You've passed." part could lead to something interesting, but not interesting enough on itself.

Also, failed test takers alter the minds of people reality around them. They are worthy of containment on themselfes, with containment procedures similar to those of powerful telepats or minor Reality Benders. Direct interviews with such entities are irresponsible and stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great concept. Reading through the first half, this almost seemed like a -J but the interview really fixed that. I fixed up some grammar but the details could still use a little tweaking. Perhaps restricting which papers this applies to a bit more would do it. Like instead of 200█ onwards, it only occurred for the years 200█-200█. Work on this, I can see it becoming a very strong article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm. Honestly, it seems like a OTT entry. If you fail this test, you fail at life! That would make a great -J, but played straight it doesn't do anything me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. I like that not everyone has something happen to them, just the best and worse. I guess that leaves us with the average.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to everyone in chat and in the forums who helped me with this.
